Arkin, Kamile

Ma, Xuesong

coredata.dc:description: Carbon dot (CD)-based room temperature afterglow (RTA) materials have attracted extensive attention due to their great application prospects in many fields. However, the afterglow lifetimes of most CD-based RTA materials with long-wavelength emission are short. Herein, a strategy has been proposed to effectively extend the lifetime of CD-based RTA materials with long-wavelength emission. It is based on the synergy of Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) and thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF). A molecule with TADF, PDINSA (2-naphthalenesulfonic-acid-substituted perylene diimide), was designed as a FRET acceptor, which was complexed with an RTA donor CDs@BA (BA: boric acid). As anticipated, a complex with an emission center at 595 nm and an afterglow lifetime of 549.69 ms was successfully constructed. This strategy can produce a synergistic effect of “1 + 1 > 2,” enabling the composite to obtain an enhanced lifetime of 1.76 times that of CDs@BA.

Schematic diagrams of the synergistic lifetime extension strategy to construct long-wavelength-emitting RTA materials with long lifetimes

(B) Highly schematic diagram of energy transfer of materials in synergistic lifetime extension strategy (M Matrix(BA), and P represents PDINSA; ∗, excited state, + and −, two electrons with opposite spin directions; 1 for singlet, 3 for triplet).

head.ce:title: Synergistic lifetime extension strategy based on Förster resonance energy transfer and TADF for carbon-dot-based room temperature afterglow

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry, Northeast Normal University, Changchun 130024, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Chemistry, Northeast Normal University, Changchun 130024, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dot (CD)-based room temperature afterglow (RTA) materials have attracted extensive attention due to their great application prospects in many fields. However, the afterglow lifetimes of most CD-based RTA materials with long-wavelength emission are short. Herein, a strategy has been proposed to effectively extend the lifetime of CD-based RTA materials with long-wavelength emission. It is based on the synergy of Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) and thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF). A molecule with TADF, PDINSA (2-naphthalenesulfonic-acid-substituted perylene diimide), was designed as a FRET acceptor, which was complexed with an RTA donor CDs@BA (BA: boric acid). As anticipated, a complex with an emission center at 595 nm and an afterglow lifetime of 549.69 ms was successfully constructed. This strategy can produce a synergistic effect of “1 + 1 > 2,” enabling the composite to obtain an enhanced lifetime of 1.76 times that of CDs@BA.

Room temperature afterglow (RTA) materials, commonly known as “night pearl,” can emit lasting light after the excitation light is removed and are widely used in night emergency instructions and anti-counterfeiting. RTA materials based on carbon dots (CDs) have recently attracted attention due to their easy synthesis. However, the lifetimes of CD-based RTA materials derived from Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) are generally shorter than those of energy donors, which limits their practical applications. Here, a synergistic lifetime extension strategy is proposed in which molecules with thermally activated delayed fluorescence property are used as acceptors in FRET and the CD materials as donors. The composite achieves a longer lifetime than those of donors. This work provides a facile and feasible strategy to construct long-lifetime, CD-based RTA materials. Perhaps soon, bright long-lifetime CD-based RTA materials can be applied to night lighting, greatly saving energy.

Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) is often used to construct carbon dot (CD)-based room temperature afterglow (RTA) materials. However, the lifetime of the obtained composites is usually shorter than that of the energy donor. A synergistic lifetime extension strategy based on FRET and thermally activated delayed fluorescence is proposed, resulting in composites with longer afterglow lifetimes than the donor. Four multicolor CD-based RTA materials are synthesized and have great potential for application in anti-counterfeiting and lighting devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: This study did not generate datasets or codes.

ce:section.ce:para:
Room temperature afterglow (RTA) materials are unique photoluminescence materials. 1 They are long-lived luminescent materials that absorb energy at room temperature and exhibit an afterglow after excitation ceases. 1 Room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) and thermally activated delayed fluorescence (TADF) belong to the afterglow category. 2
,
3
,
4 RTA materials can effectively eliminate background interference and have become a research hotspot in the fields of biological fluorescence labeling and optical information storage. 5 In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) have received great attention in many fields due to their unique optical properties. 6 Compared with pure organic RTA materials, CD-based RTA materials have advantages such as excellent afterglow performance, simple preparation, low toxicity, good stability and biocompatibility, and easy structural regulation, which provide them with great practical application prospects in light-emitting diodes (LEDs), anti-counterfeiting, and sensors. 7
,
8
,
9

CD-based RTA materials can be divided into two types: one is CDs that emit afterglow through self-protection. To obtain stable excited triplet-state states, B, N, P, and other elements are usually doped in the synthesis process of CDs. 10
,
11
,
12
,
13 He’s team synthesized a B-doped deep blue RTP CD with an emission center at 418 nm and a lifetime of up to 6.47 s. 14 Another CD-based RTA material is CDs embedded into a certain matrix to form a composite, and the matrix can assist the CDs with emitting afterglow. Some matrices, such as silica and polyvinyl alcohol, can isolate oxygen and moisture, and more importantly, they can form covalent bonds with CDs to inhibit the vibration or rotation of the surface groups of CDs, thereby producing afterglow. 15
,
16
,
17 For example, Sun and co-workers embedded CDs into SiO2 to synthesize an afterglow material with an emission wavelength of 464 nm and a lifetime of 5.72 s. 18 Other matrices such as boron oxide and urea can also serve as energy donors and transfer their own long afterglow to CDs through energy transfer. 19 Hu’s group reported an energy-transfer-mediated method in which they embedded phosphorescent CDs in an afterglow matrix (B2O3 or urea) to enhance the phosphorescence of CDs, enabling the phosphorescence lifetime of the CDs to reach 2.15 s. 19 Although various CD-based RTA materials with ultra-long lifetimes have been synthesized, the afterglow emission is mainly concentrated in the blue-green region. 11
,
20
,
21
,
22 On the contrary, some long-wavelength-emitting CD-based RTA materials have short lifetimes, which greatly limits their practical applications in advanced information security and encryption. 23 Furthermore, in the field of optoelectronic devices, especially in white LED (WLED) applications, red emission is the key to realize high-efficiency WLEDs. 23 Therefore, the development of CD-based RTA materials with long wavelength and long lifetime is of great significance.

Combining Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) with RTA has been shown to be an effective method that can endow materials with long lifetimes. 24 Recently, some researchers developed long-wavelength CD-based RTA materials by using short-wavelength CD-based RTA materials as donors and some fluorescent small molecules with long-wavelength emission as energy acceptors. For example, Liu’s team encapsulated green CDs (lifetime of 1.8 s) and rhodamine B in SiO2 and constructed a long-wavelength CD-based RTA material with an emission wavelength of 600 nm and an afterglow lifetime of 0.91 s through efficient FRET. 15 Although the emission wavelength of the materials constructed based on this strategy significantly increases, the afterglow lifetime is always shorter than that of the individual donors. 15
,
17 This means that the long lifetime of the donor has not fully migrated to the composite material.

Yan and Zhou proposed a TADF-assisted FRET strategy in the study of molecular RTP materials to enhance RTP lifetime in which TADF molecules acted as donors and RTP materials acted as acceptors. 25 Inspired by this, can we use RTA materials as energy donors and TADF molecules as energy receptors to construct long-lived CD-based RTA materials with long wavelength?

In this work, a synergistic lifetime extension strategy based on FRET and TADF was designed. The water-soluble 2-naphthalenesulfonic-acid-substituted perylene diimide (PDINSA) was used to construct a long-wavelength CD-based RTA material due to its large π-π conjugate plane, ideal excited-state lifetime, excellent photobleaching resistance, and good thermal stability. 26
,
27
,
28
,
29 PDINSA with TADF properties was selected as the FRET acceptor and CDs@BA (BA: boric acid) as the donor, and the long-wavelength-emitting CD-based RTA material PDINSA-CDs(LEV)@BA was synthesized. It achieved an enhanced afterglow lifetime that was longer than that of the donor material or the acceptor molecule (Scheme 1 ). Only after the TADF of PDINSA was activated at a specific temperature could the composite exhibit enhanced lifetime, demonstrating the effectiveness of the synergistic lifetime extension strategy. This strategy is beneficial to compensate for the short lifetime of long-wavelength-emitting CD-based RTA materials. The multicolor CD-based RTA material constructed by this strategy exhibits excellent performance in advanced anti-counterfeiting and lighting equipment applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of multicolor CD-based RTA materials
ce:section.ce:para:
CDs were synthesized using three antibiotics, pefloxacin (PEF), sarafloxacin (SAR), and levofloxacin (LEV), as precursors. The CDs were further complexed with BA to obtain three long-afterglow materials with blue, blue-green, and chartreuse RTA emissions, respectively. Figures 1A–1C show the transmission electron micrographs (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images of three antibiotic-derived CDs. The average particle diameters of CDs(PEF), CDs(SAR), and CDs(LEV) were 1.68, 1.48, and 1.63 nm, respectively. It can be observed from HRTEMs that CDs(PEF), CDs(SAR), and CDs(LEV) all contain lattice fringes of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of graphitized carbon. 1
,
30 The TEMs and HRTEMs of CDs@BA are shown in Figure S1. CDs(PEF), CDs(SAR), and CDs(LEV) were uniformly dispersed and aggregated in the matrix formed by heating BA. The average particle sizes of CDs(PEF), CDs(SAR), and CDs(LEV) in the matrix were 6.3, 3.2, and 6.7 nm, respectively, and their 0.21 nm lattice spacing can also be observed. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra of CDs(PEF), CDs(SAR), and CDs(LEV) presented a broad peak at 24° (002) (shown in Figure 1D), demonstrating that the three CDs had graphene structure. 31
,
32 The XRD spectra of CDs(PEF)@BA, CDs(SAR)@BA, and CDs(LEV)@BA are shown in Figure 1E. Broad peaks belonging to CDs were also observed. Meanwhile, they all contained sharp peaks at 14.5°, 27.8°, and 39.9°, which were attributed to B2O3 in matrix. 2
,
33 The X-ray electron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra (Figure 1F) show that CDs(PEF)@BA, CDs(SAR)@BA, and CDs(LEV)@BA contain B (193.0 eV), C (285.0 eV), N (398.4 eV), and O (531.8 eV) elements. Figure S2 displays the high-resolution XPS spectra for B 1s. The B 1s band can be deconvoluted into three binding energy peaks at 192.9, 193.8, and 194.8 eV, corresponding to BCO2, B2O3, and B–O, respectively. 19
,
30
,
33 In CDs(PEF)@BA and CDs(SAR)@BA, the BCO2 peak is the strongest, followed by B2O3 and B–O bonds. In CDs(LEV)@BA, B2O3 is the main form, and the peak intensities of BCO2 and B–O are slightly weaker and similar. The C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s band analyses are presented in Figure S3. The presence of sp2 C (C=C, 284.0 eV), sp3 C (C–O/C–N, 284.8 eV), and –C=O/–COOH (286.0 eV) were confirmed. 33
,
34 The N 1s band indicated that the small amount of N atoms in CDs(PEF)@BA, CDs(SAR)@BA, and CDs(LEV)@BA existed in the form of graphitic N (401.5 eV), which was caused by the precursor in the forming process of CDs. 35 The O 1s band can be fitted with three peaks at 531.8, 532.7, and 533.5 eV for C=O, B2O3, and C–OH, respectively. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of CDs(PEF)@BA, CDs(SAR)@BA, and CDs(LEV)@BA are shown in Figure S4. The stretching vibration peaks of O–H (3,240 cm−1) and B–O (1,455 and 1,193 cm−1) can be observed, which is consistent with the results of XRD and XPS analyses. 2
,
33

The ultraviolet-visible (UV-vis) absorption spectra of CDs(PEF)@BA, CDs(SAR)@BA, and CDs(LEV)@BA are shown in Figure 1G. This indicates that they have intense absorption for UV light and blue-green light. Compared with the absorption spectra of CDs(PEF), CDs(SAR), CDs(LEV), and Matrix(BA) (shown in Figure S5), Matrix(BA) had weak absorption ability for visible light above 400 nm. Three CDs were dark brown under visible light and had strong absorption for visible light above 400 nm. When CDs were uniformly dispersed in Matrix(BA), the color of CDs@BA was much lighter than the corresponding CDs, so the absorption band edge cutoff was about 500 nm. The main source of light absorption capacity of CDs@BA was CDs. From the RTA emission spectra under different excitation wavelengths and the RTA excitation spectra of CDs(PEF)@BA, CDs(SAR)@BA, and CDs(LEV)@BA (Figure S6), we found that their optimal excitation wavelengths were all centered at 400 nm. Figure S7 shows the fluorescence spectra of three precursors, three CDs, and three CDs@BA at their optimal excitation wavelengths. When excited by 352, 345, and 389 nm, PEF, SAR, and LEV can emit fluorescence with centers located at 438, 444, and 498 nm, respectively. CDs(PEF), CDs(SAR), and CDs(LEV) exhibited fluorescence emission peaks at 463, 481, and 518 nm, respectively, under 360 nm excitation. After binding with BA, the fluorescence emission peaks of CDs(PEF)@BA, CDs(SAR)@BA, and CDs(LEV)@BA under 400 nm excitation were almost identical to the corresponding CDs. Same as the fluorescence emission, their RTA emissions were centered at 461, 483, and 518 nm, respectively (shown in Figure 1H). The time-resolved emission spectra (TRESs) of CDs(PEF)@BA, CDs(SAR)@BA, and CDs(LEV)@BA shown in Figure S8 indicated that their afterglow emission wavelengths did not change with time. According to the afterglow decay curves of the three CDs@BA (Figure 1I), their afterglow lifetimes were 145.27, 113.62, and 311.78 ms, respectively (recorded at λem = 461, 483, and 518 nm, respectively). The fluorescence lifetimes of CDs(PEF), CDs(SAR), and CDs(LEV) (shown in Figure S9) were only 3.93, 4.87, and 7.03 ns, respectively.

To explore the RTA mechanism of CDs(PEF)@BA, CDs(SAR)@BA, and CDs(LEV)@BA, the RTA emission spectra of three precursors and three CDs were investigated (shown in Figure S10). Three precursors (PEF, SAR, and LEV) and three CDs (CDs(PEF), CDs(SAR), and CDs(LEV)) had almost no RTA emission properties. Figure S11 shows that Matrix(BA) has a fluorescence emission at 460 nm and a dual-wavelength RTA emission at 469 and 526 nm under 400 nm excitation. Its afterglow lifetime reached 353.80 ms at 526 nm emission. Therefore, the RTA characteristics of the three CDs@BA were derived from Matrix(BA) rather than CDs.

The RTA emissions of CDs(PEF)@BA, CDs(SAR)@BA, and CDs(LEV)@BA were speculated to originate from the efficient triplet-to-singlet FRET (TS-FRET) between CDs and Matrix(BA). 36
,
37 In this TS-FRET system, Matrix(BA) acted as an energy donor, and CDs(PEF), CDs(SAR), and CDs(LEV) acted as energy acceptors, respectively. First, the distance between CDs and Matrix(BA) was closed (as shown in TEM), and the RTA emission spectrum of Matrix(BA) overlapped with the excitation and absorption spectra of CDs in a large area (as shown in Figures S12A and S12B), which established the foundation for the energy transfer between the Matrix(BA) and CDs. Second, the afterglow decay curves of CDs@BA at Matrix(BA) emission wavelengths were monitored (Figure S12C), and the lifetimes of CDs(PEF)@BA, CDs(SAR)@BA, and CDs(LEV)@BA were 55.76, 25.33, and 46.45 ms, respectively. The afterglow lifetime of the matrix(BA) in these three composite materials is much smaller than that of the matrix(BA) itself (353.80 ms), indicating that the matrix(BA) transfers energy to CDs. Third, according to the emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths and the excitation spectra of CDs (Figures S13A–S13D), the optimal excitation wavelength of the three CDs was 360 nm. The optimal fluorescence and RTA excitation wavelengths of Matrix(BA) were 400 nm (Figures S13E and S13F), which was the same as those of CDs(PEF)@BA, CDs(SAR)@BA, and CDs(LEV)@BA but different from that of CDs. This showed that in the three CDs@BA, the emission peaks of the acceptor CDs were excited by the optimal excitation wavelength of the donor Matrix(BA). The above results indicated that the multicolor long-lifetime RTA emissions of CDs(PEF)@BA, CDs(SAR)@BA, and CDs(LEV)@BA originated from the effective TS-FRET between Matrix(BA) and multicolor CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synergistic lifetime extension strategy for CD-based RTA materials with long wavelength
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain CD-based RTA materials with long-wavelength emission and long lifetime, a synergistic lifetime extension strategy based on FRET and TADF was designed. A molecule with the property of TADF as the acceptor for FRET is required firstly. Here, based on our previous work, PDINSA with large π-electron-conjugated structure and excellent photothermal stability were selected as acceptors. 38 To investigate the excited-state characteristics of the PDINSA molecule, theoretical calculations were performed on its excited singlet and triplet energy levels, shown in Figures 2 A and 2B. The energy-level difference between S1 and T2 of PDINSA was only 0.036 eV, and the spin-orbit coupling matrix element (SOCME) for S1→T2 was 3.380 cm−1, indicating that the thermally activated intersystem crossing (ISC) process occurred from S1 to T2. 39 For the reverse ISC (RISC) process, the energy-level difference between S1 and T1 of PDINSA was as high as 1.174 eV, which made RISC from T1 to S1 almost impossible in theory. The highest SOCME between T2 and S1 implied the RISC process from T2 to S1, which was not a thermal activation process because the energy of T2 was higher than that of S1. Significantly, the energy differences between S2, S3 and T2, T3 was calculated to be between 0.006 and 0.014 eV, which indicated that PDINSA might undergo high-level RISC (hRISC). 40 Since the energy levels of S2 and S3 were higher than those of T2 and T3, this hRISC was also considered to be a thermally activated process. By comparing the SOCMEs between them, it was found that the SOCME between T2 and S3 was the largest, reaching 1.250 cm−1. According to the above results, PDINSA might have TADF property, which originated from the ISC from S1 to T2 and the subsequent RISC from T2 to S3. The above calculations were based on the boat-form PDINSA model, and we also conducted the same calculations using the chair-form PDINSA model. The corresponding natural transition orbital (NTO) diagram and calculation results are listed in Figure S14. Although there are some differences in specific energy levels, the TADF property still existed, which originated from the ISC from S1 to T2, T3 and the subsequent RISC from T2, T3 to S3.

Another key to the synergistic life extension strategy is to construct an effective FRET system. Based on the overlap of the RTA emission spectrum of CDs(LEV)@BA and the fluorescence excitation spectrum of PDINSA (Figure S15A), CDs(LEV)@BA were selected as the energy donor for FRET. The emission spectra of composite PDINSA-CDs(LEV)@BA (shown in Figure S15B) under different excitation wavelengths and the RTA excitation spectrum (shown in Figure S15C) showed that its optimal excitation wavelength was 400 nm, which was consistent with Matrix(BA). Furthermore, PDINSA-CDs(LEV)@BA monitored at the emission wavelength of the donor (518 nm) was shortened to 273.41 ms (Figure S15D). The emission intensity of RTA belonging to the donor CDs(LEV)@BA was greatly attenuated, whereas that of the acceptor PDINSA was enhanced (Figure 2C). These results indicated the successful fabrication of the dual-FRET system. As expected, PDINSA-CDs(LEV)@BA obtained an optimal emission wavelength centered at 595 nm (shown in Figure 2D). This is derived from the strong visible light absorption characteristics of PDINSA and its ability to generate 592 nm fluorescence emission under an optimal excitation wavelength of 537 nm (Figures S16A and S16B). It is worth mentioning that the fluorescence intensity of PDINSA under excitation at 400 nm was very weak, further indicating that effective FRET had occurred (Figure S16C). The afterglow decay curve of PDINSA-CDs(LEV)@BA is shown in Figure 2E, and the afterglow lifetime is as long as 549.69 ms, which is longer than that of the donor CDs(LEV)@BA (311.78 ms). Figure 2F shows the TRES of PDINSA-CDs(LEV)@BA. The afterglow emission of PDINSA-CDs(LEV)@BA was always centered at 595 nm, and it still had a certain emission intensity when the excitation stopped for 450 ms. The above results indicated that the synthesis of long-lifetime CD-based RTA materials using the synergistic lifetime extension strategy was successful.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization and photophysical properties of PDINSA-CDs(LEV)@BA
ce:section.ce:para: From the XRD spectra of PDINSA@BA and PDINSA-CDs(LEV)@BA (Figure S17A), it can be found that they contained the same B2O3 composition as CDs(LEV)@BA. The FT-IR spectrum shows that PDINSA-CDs(LEV)@BA contain hydroxyl groups and B–O bonds (Figure S17B). The TEMs of PDINSA-CDs(LEV) and PDINSA-CDs(LEV)@BA are shown in Figure S18. The average particle size of the PDINSA in the dispersed state was 6.0 nm with a lattice spacing of 0.37 nm, which was caused by the π-π stacking of PDINSA. 38 CDs(LEV) in PDINSA-CDs(LEV) maintained their own average particle size of 1.63 nm and lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, indicating that PDINSA did not cause the aggregation of CDs(LEV). The TEM of PDINSA-CDs(LEV)@BA showed that CDs(LEV) and PDINSA were uniformly dispersed in Matrix(BA). The average particle size of PDINSA reached 10.1 nm, and a lattice spacing of 0.37 nm can also be observed. The average particle size of CDs(LEV) in PDINSA-CDs(LEV)@BA was about 6.7 nm, which was consistent with that of CDs(LEV)@BA. The XPS spectra show that, in addition to containing the same bond as CDs(LEV)@BA, PDINSA-CDs(LEV)@BA also have a sulfonic acid group (R–SO3H, 169.90 eV), which is derived from PDINSA (Figure S19). The fluorescence spectra of PDINSA@BA are presented in Figures S20A and S20B. It completely retained the fluorescence properties of PDINSA (Figure S16B). The optimal excitation wavelength of PDINSA@BA was different from that of Matrix(BA) (400 nm), which confirmed that TS-FRET did not occur between PDINSA and Matrix(BA). Although the fluorescence excitation spectrum of PDINSA overlapped with the RTA emission spectrum of Matrix(BA), their complex, PDINSA@BA, had almost no afterglow emission at a delayed time of 25 ms (Figures S20C and S20D). This proved that the afterglow emission of PDINSA-CDs(LEV)@BA was due to CDs(LEV) becoming the bridge of FRET, connecting Matrix(BA) and PDINSA successfully. It also demonstrated that effective FRET was a key factor in the synergistic lifetime extension strategy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Afterglow mechanism of CDs@BA and PDINSA-CDs(LEV)@BA
ce:section.ce:para: Usually, energy loss is unavoidable during the FRET process between donor and acceptor, resulting in a shorter lifetime of the complex than that of the donor. The synergistic lifetime extension strategy utilizes TADF molecules as acceptors. Therefore, energy comes not only from the donor but also from heat, which is why the lifetime is extended. To better explain the design idea of this strategy, Scheme 1B shows a highly schematic diagram of energy transfer to illustrate the lifetime extension mechanism. Acceptor molecules PDINSA with TADF property generate singlet excitons after receiving the energy transferred by the donor. After the ISC and hRISC processes, the excitons return to the singlet state and then transition back to the ground state and release energy. Due to the spin flipping of excitons, the PDINSA molecules undergo a cascade process of S1→T2→S3→S1→S0 instead of the direct S1→S0. This process of energy transfer combined with TADF can extend the RTA lifetime.

The temperature-dependent afterglow decay curves of CDs(PEF)@BA, CDs(SAR)@BA, CDs(LEV)@BA. and PDINSA-CDs(LEV)@BA were obtained from 80 to 300 K, as shown in Figures 3A–3D. All four materials had the longest average afterglow lifetime at 80 K. As the temperature increased, the afterglow lifetime gradually shortened. This was due to the enhanced non-radiative transition of the triplet excitons of Matrix(BA) at high temperature, 5
,
18 further indicating that the afterglow of the four materials originated from the phosphorescence of Matrix(BA). 41
,
42 The average lifetimes at different temperature of the four materials are summarized in Figure 3E. With the increase of temperature, the trends of the average lifetimes of CDs(PEF)@BA, CDs(SAR)@BA, and CDs(LEV)@BA were almost the same. For PDINSA-CDs(LEV)@BA, the lifetime was always less than that of CDs(LEV)@BA at temperatures lower than 250 K. This is because there was an energy loss in the process of energy transfer from CDs(LEV)@BA to PDINSA-CDs(LEV)@BA through dual FRET; thus, the lifetime of the complex was shorter than that of the donor. When the temperature was higher than 250 K, the afterglow lifetime of PDINSA-CDs(LEV)@BA was longer than that of CDs(LEV)@BA. Moreover, the average lifetime difference between PDINSA-CDs(LEV)@BA and CDs(LEV)@BA was further expanded when the temperature reached 300 K. The temperature-dependent afterglow decay curves (80–300 K) of PDINSA@BA were recorded and are shown in Figure 3F. The curves can be divided into two components: where the prompt component was the fluorescence emission, and where the delayed component was attributed to the delayed fluorescence of the PDINSA molecule. 43 When the temperature gradually increased from 250 to 300 K (illustration of Figure 3F), the intensity of the delayed fluorescence gradually increased, which was due to the thermal activation of the RISC from the excited triplet state to the excited singlet state. 44
,
45 As can be seen from Figure S21, the intensity of steady-state spectra of PDINSA@BA gradually decreased with increasing temperature. However, the afterglow intensity gradually increased with the increase of temperature and rapidly increased above 250 K (as shown in Figures S21A–S21C). This is consistent with the results of temperature-dependent transient spectra. Further, it indicated that PDINSA had TADF property and was activated at 250 K. The afterglow of PDINSA-CDs(LEV)@BA originated from the triplet phosphorescence of Matrix(BA), and the intensities of its steady-state and afterglow spectra (Figures S21D and S21E) gradually decreased with increasing temperature. However, the decline of relative intensity (I/I80K) above 250 K was flattening. By comparing the relative lifetimes of CDs(LEV)@BA and PDINSA-CDs(LEV)@BA (Figure S21F), it can be found that activating the TADF performance of PDINSA can improve the afterglow strength and lifetime of the composite material. Figures 3G and 3H show the afterglow mechanism of CDs@BA and PDINSA-CDs(LEV)@BA. For CDs@BA (Figure 3G), the energy was transferred from Matrix(BA) to CDs through TS-FRET; thus, CDs@BA produced an afterglow corresponding to the emission wavelength of CDs at the optimal excitation wavelength of Matrix(BA). For PDINSA-CDs(LEV)@BA, through dual FRET, PDINSA-CDs(LEV)@BA produced an afterglow corresponding to the PDINSA emission wavelength at the optimal excitation wavelength of Matrix(BA). When the temperature was lower than 250 K, the lifetime of PDINSA-CDs(LEV)@BA was shorter than that of CDs(LEV)@BA due to the loss in the energy transfer process. When the temperature was higher than 250 K, the TADF property of PDINSA was activated. PDINSA excited by dual FRET was in a singlet state, a part of which underwent ISC from S1 to T2 and hRISC from T2 to S3 to generate delayed fluorescence. This enabled PDINSA-CDs(LEV)@BA not only to emit long-wavelength afterglow but also caused the afterglow lifetime to be longer than that of the donor CDs(LEV)@BA at the same temperature. The enhanced lifetime of the composite was achieved only after the TADF properties of PDINSA were triggered at 250 K, further confirming the effectiveness of the synergistic lifetime extension strategy. This strategy may be a new effective idea for constructing long-lived RTA materials. Compared with previous works, the afterglow lifetime of CD-based RTA materials synthesized using this synergistic strategy was considerable (Table 1 ). Compared with the long-wavelength CD-based RTA materials constructed by FRET, the material obtained by the synergistic lifetime extension strategy is special in that it has longer lifetime than the donor. Additionally, compared with other methods, the material synthesized by this strategy also has longer afterglow lifetime and comparable emission wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Properties of the four CD-based RTA materials and their application in advanced anti-counterfeiting and lighting
ce:section.ce:para:
The four multicolor RTA materials, CDs(PEF)@BA, CDs(SAR)@BA, CDs(LEV)@BA, and PDINSA-CDs(LEV)@BA, have been successfully obtained. The photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQYs) of CDs(PEF)@BA, CDs(SAR)@BA, CDs(LEV)@BA, and PDINSA-CDs(LEV)@BA were 52.3%, 42.2%, 49.8%, and 13.1%, respectively. Their phosphorescence quantum yields (PhQYs) were 35.1%, 26.5%, 19.2%, and 9.7%, respectively. Their International Commission on Illumination (CIE) color coordinates were (0.16, 0.2), (0.17, 0.32), (0.30, 0.57), and (0.42, 0.42), respectively, as shown in Figure S22. The afterglow emission spectra of complexes containing different concentrations of the acceptor and the afterglow decay curves of the complex recorded at the donor emission wavelength are shown in Figure S23. As the acceptor concentration increased, the afterglow intensity of the complex increased. Simultaneously, the lifetime of the donor gradually shortened as the concentration of the acceptor increased. The energy transfer efficiency E and the Föster distance R 0 were calculated according to the following equations 15
,

The E of CDs(PEF)@BA, CDs(SAR)@BA, CDs(LEV)@BA, and PDINSA-CDs(LEV)@BA are 0.8424, 0.9284, 0.8687, and 0.1231, respectively. The R 0 of CDs(PEF)@BA, CDs(SAR)@BA, CDs(LEV)@BA, and PDINSA-CDs(LEV)@BA are 1.42, 2.36, 2.06, and 4.10 nm, respectively. The digital photographs before and after turning off UV light and the daylight lamp of multicolor RTA materials are exhibited in Figure 4 . After the UV light was turned off, an afterglow lasting 2–4 s can be observed with the naked eye (shown in Figure 4A and Video S1). More importantly, the four multicolor RTA materials can be excited by daylight lamp to emit afterglow. As shown in Figure 4B and Video S2, CDs(PEF)@BA, CDs(SAR)@BA, and CDs(LEV)@BA can emit an afterglow of about 0.5 s after the daylight lamp is turned off, while PDINSA-CDs(LEV)@BA can generate an afterglow lasting more than 4 s.

ce:e-component.ce:label: Video S1. Demonstration video of room temperature afterglow of four multicolor CD-based RTA materials (excited by UV light)
Video S2. Demonstration video of room temperature afterglow of four multicolor CD-based RTA materials (excited by daylight lamp)

ce:section.ce:para: The photobleaching resistance properties of the four multicolor RTA materials were investigated and are shown in Figure S24. CDs(PEF)@BA, CDs(SAR)@BA, and CDs(LEV)@BA maintained 80%, 75%, and 86% of their afterglow intensities, respectively, after continuous excitation for 60 min. Matrix(BA) maintained more than 99% of its afterglow intensity (Figure S25). The afterglow intensities of CDs(PEF)@BA, CDs(SAR)@BA, and CDs(LEV)@BA decreased with continuous excitation, possibly due to CDs maintaining a singlet excited state under continuous excitation, which can react with oxygen and undergo permanent structural changes, resulting in a decrease in afterglow intensity with continuous excitation. 53 PDINSA-CDs(LEV)@BA had excellent photobleaching resistance properties and could maintain 99% of the afterglow intensity after being continuously excited for 60 min. There were two possible reasons. One was that CDs(LEV), as the medium of dual FRET, were in the excited singlet state for a short time. After being excited by the first TS-FRET, CDs(LEV) immediately underwent the second FRET to transfer energy to PDINSA. Another reason was that PDINSA was a stable molecule with a large conjugated structure, and when it was in the excited state, it did not tend to react with oxygen. Figure S26 shows the effect of storage time on the afterglow intensities of CDs(PEF)@BA, CDs(SAR)@BA, CDs(LEV)@BA, and PDINSA-CDs(LEV)@BA. It can be found that the afterglow intensities of the four RTA materials did not decrease significantly within 30 days, and they could maintain 98%, 88%, 93%, and 100%, respectively, of their initial afterglow intensities. Simultaneously, their afterglow lifetimes maintained 94%, 100%, 90%, and 100% of the initial values, respectively. This indicates that the four RTA materials have good time stability.

Based on the excellent optical properties of the four RTA materials, their application in the field of advanced anti-counterfeiting and encryption was explored, as shown in Figure 5 . In Figures 5A and 5B, the materials used in the non-afterglow parts were a mixture of precursors and BA dried at 60°C, and the parts that needed to be encrypted were composed of CDs@BA and PDINSA-CDs(LEV)@BA. When the UV light was turned on, it showed “NO CHEM NO FUTURE,” and when the UV lamp was turned off, the encrypted part of the password “NENU” was left, as shown in Figure 5A. Because of the differences in the afterglow lifetimes of the four materials, the letter “N” spelled by PDINSA-CDs(LEV)@BA and the letter “U” composed of CDs(LEV)@BA will be left over time, which realizes dynamic encryption. Furthermore, since PDINSA-CDs(LEV)@BA can emit persistent afterglow after being excited by daylight lamps, an anti-counterfeiting method excited by different light sources was designed, as shown in Figure 5B and Video S3. When the light source was turned on, all patterns in the picture could be seen. When the UV light was turned off, four small fishes drawn by multicolor RTA materials all emitted afterglow; after turning off the daylight lamp, only a small red fish drawn by PDINSA-CDs(LEV)@BA emitted a persistent bright afterglow. Figure 5C demonstrates the application of CDs(LEV)@BA on quick response (QR) code encryption. A QR code drawn by CDs(LEV)@BA still maintained a clear afterglow after the UV light was turned off, and the correct information can be obtained by scanning the code with a mobile phone.

ce:e-component.ce:label: Video S3. Demonstration video of graphic security made from multicolor CD-based RTA materials

ce:section.ce:para: The emission wavelengths of the four multicolor RTA materials covered almost the entire visible light region, based on which two types of WLEDs, cool white and warm white, were constructed, as shown in Figure 6 . Both WLEDs emitted about 2 s afterglow after the power was turned off. In the cool WLED, the amount of CDs(PEF)@BA and CDs(SAR)@BA is higher than CDs(LEV)@BA and PDINSA-CDs(LEV)@BA, leading to the generation of turquoise afterglow.More PDINSA-CDs(LEV)@BA were added into the warm WLED, which made the afterglow appear orange-red. The CIE color coordinates of the cool WLED were (0.31, 0.32) with a correlated color temperature (CCT) of 6933 K. The CIE color coordinates of the warm WLED were (0.41, 0.39), and its CCT was 3,369 K. It is worth mentioning that both WLEDs had a positive color rendering index (Ra), among which the Ra of the cool WLED reached 90 and the Ra of the warm WLED was 88.

In conclusion, a synergistic lifetime extension strategy based on FRET and TADF was designed. On the basis of multicolor CD-based RTA materials obtained by the interaction of antibiotics and BA, PDINSA molecules with TADF were introduced as FRET acceptors. Through this strategy, a long-wavelength RTA material with an emission center of 595 nm and an afterglow lifetime of 549.69 ms was successfully constructed. The effective FRET between the donor (CD-based RTA material) and the acceptor (PDINSA) had a synergistic effect with the TADF of PDINSA, leading to a composite with an enhanced lifetime up to 1.76 times that of the donor. The results showed that the composite exhibited a longer lifetime only after the TADF of PDINSA was activated at a specific temperature, demonstrating the effectiveness of the synergistic lifetime extension strategy. The obtained four multicolor RTA materials with excellent afterglow properties can be applied in advanced anti-counterfeiting and lighting equipment. This study provides a new strategy for constructing composite materials with long wavelength and long afterglow lifetime.
Further information and requests for resources and reagents should be directed to and will be fulfilled by the lead contact, Qingkun Shang (shangqk995@nenu.edu.cn).
All structural data and information generated in this study are available from the lead contact, Qingkun Shang (shangqk995@nenu.edu.cn).

where A and B represent the integral areas of total PL and phosphorescence spectra, respectively. The phosphorescence was separated from PL in a time-gated scheme. 54
,
55

47 mg PEF was dissolved in 10 mL water, and 100 μL phosphoric acid was added to help dissolve. 1 g BA was added to the above solution and was uniformly dispersed by ultrasound for 3 min. The beaker containing this mixture was wrapped with tin foil (keeping the beaker open), placed in an oven, and heated at 180°C for 6 h to obtain CDs(PEF)@BA. The process of obtaining CDs(SAR)@BA and CDs(LEV)@BA was the same as that of CDs(PEF)@BA, and the raw materials were SAR (42 mg) and LEV (40 mg), respectively. CDs were obtained without adding BA in the synthesis process. Matrix(BA) was obtained without adding antibiotic precursor and phosphoric acid in the synthesis process.

Water-soluble PDINSA was synthesized according to the published work of our research group. 38 Specifically, 0.392 g perylene 3,4,9,10-tetracarboxylic dianhydride (PTCDA), 2.232 g 5-amino-2-naphthalenesulfonic acid, 0.367 g zinc acetate, and 18 g imidazole were added to a round-bottom flask (250 mL). Under the protection of nitrogen, the round-bottom flask was placed in an oil bath at 120°C for 5 h. After that, the solution was quickly poured into a beaker containing 100 mL HCl (12 mol/L), 100 mL absolute ethanol, and 200 mL deionized water. After stirring for 12 h, the mixture was centrifuged. The precipitation was washed with absolute ethanol three times and dried in an oven at 60°C for 10 h to obtain PDINSA. 2 mg PDINSA and 40 mg LEV were dissolved in 10 mL water. 1 g BA and 100 μL phosphoric acid were added to the solution, and the mixture was dispersed by ultrasound. The beaker containing the mixture was wrapped with tin foil (keeping the beaker open) and heated in an oven at 180°C for 6 h to obtain PDINSA-CDs(LEV)@BA. PDINSA@BA was obtained without adding LEV during synthesis. The schematic diagram of the synthesis path is shown in Figure S27.

Density functional theory (DFT) calculations were performed using ORCA program suite 5.0.3. 56 All structures were optimized at the B3LYP-D3 level with the def2-SVPbasis set. For time-dependent DFT (TD-DFT) calculations, the excitation energies for the singlet and triplet states and the SOCM were evaluated at the same level of theory. The NTOs were computed with Multiwfn 3.8, and the isosurface plots for NTOs were generated by VMD 1.9.3. 57
,
58
